title: Water Ration Dispute
desc: Two families, one allocation. The math doesn't work.

steps.text:
Water allocation day at the dew farm. Every household receives a measured portion based on family size, profession, and need. The system works because everyone trusts the water-counter. Today, the water-counter is sick, and you've been asked to fill in.

The ledger is straightforward — names, household sizes, allocated portions. You measure water into clay vessels using calibrated scoops. It's going smoothly until two families arrive at the same time, both claiming the same allocation slot.

"Slot fourteen is ours. It's always been ours."

"Slot fourteen was reassigned after your cousin moved to the oasis. Check the ledger."

You check the ledger. It's been corrected in two different handwritings. Slot fourteen is claimed by both families, and neither correction has an official seal.

options.label: Split the allocation evenly between both families

steps.text:
"Half each. It's the fairest option until the water-counter can review it properly."

Neither family is happy. Half a water allocation in the desert isn't a minor inconvenience — it's the difference between comfort and careful rationing. But neither family can prove exclusive claim, and they both know it.

The smaller family — three members — accepts grudgingly. "Half is enough for us. Barely. We'll manage until the counter rules."

The larger family — six members, including two children — pushes back harder. "Half ration for six people? We'll be dry by day three."

You do the math. They're right. Half of slot fourteen isn't enough for six. You check the reserves — the emergency buffer that exists for exactly these situations. You allocate a supplementary quarter-ration from the buffer, enough to bridge the gap.

"This comes out of emergency stock. It's a loan, not a gift. The water-counter will reconcile it when she's back."

Both families leave with enough water. The emergency buffer is slightly reduced. You note everything in the ledger, in your own handwriting, with today's date. The water-counter will sort it out. Your job is to make sure nobody goes dry in the meantime.

options.label: Close the ledger and continue the day's work

steps.text:
The two corrections in the ledger are telling. The first is in blue ink — the water-counter's standard color. It reassigns slot fourteen to the second family, dated three weeks ago. The second correction is in black ink, undoing the reassignment, with no date and no seal.

Someone altered the ledger. You can't prove who, but the blue-ink correction has the counter's characteristic handwriting. The black-ink correction doesn't match anyone authorized.

You show both families. The second family — the blue-ink assignees — is vindicated. The first family goes quiet.

"We... may have asked a friend to look into it," the first family's elder admits. "We didn't think it would come to this."

You allocate slot fourteen to the second family, per the blue-ink correction. The first family's original allocation — slot eleven, smaller but legitimate — is still available. They take it without argument.

You flag the ledger tampering for the water-counter with a sealed note. Water records are serious — in the desert, falsifying water claims is treated more severely than theft.

The elder catches your arm as they leave. "We weren't trying to steal. We just... it's been a dry season. We were scared." Fear doesn't excuse tampering, but it explains it. You note that too.

The rest of allocation day proceeds without incident. Every family gets their water. The ledger is accurate again.

options.label: Finish the allocations and seal the ledger
